system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses to the same brief. They are labeled E1 through E5.

**E1:** I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data** as the primary policy. For a consumer health app, the strategic asset is trust. Users are sharing highly sensitive health information, and an AI coach will likely process data that feels even more intimate than ordinary app telemetry: symptoms, routines, medications, goals, anxieties, and potentially inferred conditions.

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option D — Cloud with opt-in training only

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, I recommend **Option D** as the primary policy. The decision hinges on a structural fact about health data: it is among the most regulated and trust-sensitive categories of personal information (HIPAA-adjacent in the US, special category data under GDPR, and similarly protected in most jurisdictions). At the same time, AI coaching is a capability-sensitive feature — a model stuck at 60-70% of frontier performance will give worse health guidance, which has its own e...

**E3:** As an analyst, I recommend **Option B: Encrypted cloud with no training** as the primary data policy. In the health-tech space, trust is not a soft metric—it is the entire business model. Users are sharing some of their most intimate, vulnerable data for the promise of better well-being.

**E4:** **Recommendation: Option A (Local-only processing)**

For a direct-to-consumer health app, privacy is not merely a compliance checkbox; it is the core product promise. I recommend adopting **local-only processing** as the primary data policy. By ensuring that no health data leaves the user's device, the company transforms privacy from a contractual assurance into a structural certainty—eliminating breach risk, subpoena exposure, and cross-border data governance headaches in a single architectural decision.

**E5:** I strongly recommend **Option D (Cloud with explicit opt-in training)** as the primary data policy for the new AI-powered health coaching feature. For a direct-to-consumer app with 2 million active users, this approach optimally balances product capability, unit economics, regulatory compliance, and user trust. Health data is intimately personal and highly regulated.

Your task: for each of the following triads of 3 responses, identify a single bipolar construct that distinguishes them. Specifically, identify how TWO of the three are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the THIRD. Name the two poles of that dimension.

Triads to analyze:
Triad 1: E1, E2, E5
Triad 2: E1, E4, E5
Triad 3: E1, E3, E5

For each triad, output EXACTLY this format with no markdown, no bold, no bullets, no numbered lists:
Triad 1:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Triad 2:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Triad 3:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Be specific. Avoid generic dichotomies like 'good vs bad'. Use plain text labels (Triad N, Left pole, Right pole) without any markdown formatting.
raw_response_content:
Triad 1:
Left pole: Permit training with user opt-in
Right pole: Prohibit training on user data

Triad 2:
Left pole: Cloud-based processing
Right pole: On-device processing only

Triad 3:
Left pole: No model training on health data
Right pole: Training with explicit user consent

message.content:
Triad 1:
Left pole: Permit training with user opt-in
Right pole: Prohibit training on user data

Triad 2:
Left pole: Cloud-based processing
Right pole: On-device processing only

Triad 3:
Left pole: No model training on health data
Right pole: Training with explicit user consent
message.reasoning:
We are asked to analyze three triads of responses. For each triad, we need to find a bipolar construct where two responses are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the third. The output should be in a specific plain text format with left and right poles, no markdown, no bold, no bullets.

First, let's recap the responses:
E1: Recommends Option B (encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data). Emphasizes trust.
E2: Recommends Option D (cloud with opt-in training only). Emphasizes balancing performance and privacy, notes structural fact about health data regulation, and that AI coaching needs high performance.
E3: Recommends Option B (encrypted cloud with no training). Emphasizes trust as the entire business model.
E4: Recommends Option A (local-only processing). Privacy as core product promise, structural certainty.
E5: Recommends Option D (cloud with explicit opt-in training). Balances product capability, economics, compliance, trust.

Now, analyze each triad:

Triad 1: E1, E2, E5.
E1: Option B (cloud, no training)
E2: Option D (cloud, opt-in training)
E5: Option D (cloud, opt-in training)

So E2 and E5 both recommend Option D, which involves cloud processing and opt-in training. E1 recommends Option B, which is cloud processing but no training. So the similarity between E2 and E5 is that they allow training on user data (with opt-in), while E1 prohibits any training. So the bipolar construct could be about training on user data versus no training. But we need to phrase it as a dimension where two are similar and one is different. So Left pole: "Prohibit training on user health data" (E1), Right pole: "Allow training with user opt-in" (E2, E5). Alternatively, "Data never used for model training" vs "Data used for training with consent". But I need to make sure the labels are short (max 12 words).

Triad 2: E1, E4, E5.
E1: Cloud encrypted, no training (Option B)
E4: Local-only processing (Option A)
E5: Cloud with opt-in training (Option D)

Here, E1 and E5 both involve cloud processing (data leaves device), while E4 is local-only (no data leaves device). So the similarity is between E1 and E5: both use cloud processing. E4 uses only local processing. So the bipolar construct: cloud processing vs local processing. Or "Data processed in cloud" vs "Data processed only on device".

Triad 3: E1, E3, E5.
E1: Option B (cloud, no training)
E3: Option B (cloud, no training)
E5: Option D (cloud, opt-in training)

E1 and E3 both recommend the same option, B, with no training. E5 recommends D, with opt-in training. So again, the distinguishing factor is training. So similar to Triad 1. But Triad 3 explicitly includes two identical recommendations (E1 and E3 both Option B) and one different (E5 Option D). So the bipolar construct: "No training on health data" vs "Training with user permission". But we need to be specific. The dimension is about whether the data is used for training the AI model. E1 and E3 are against any training; E5 allows it with opt-in.

We must output exactly the format with left and right poles. Be careful: In each triad, we identify which two are similar and which one is different. For Triad 1, E2&E5 similar, E1 different. For Triad 2, E1&E5 similar (cloud), E4 different (local). For Triad 3, E1&E3 similar (no training), E5 different (opt-in training). So label accordingly. The left pole is the attribute of the two, the right pole is the attribute of the one? But the instruction says: "identify how TWO of the three are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the THIRD. Name the two poles of that dimension." So the dimension has two ends; one end is the attribute shared by the two, the other end is the attribute of the third. So I should put the shared attribute as one pole, and the distinct attribute as the opposite pole. Which is left and which is right doesn't matter as long as they are labeled. I'll be consistent: put the shared two on the left pole, and the singleton on the right pole.

Triad 1: Shared (E2, E5): opt-in training allowed. Distinct (E1): no training. So left pole: "Training with user consent" or "Allow training with opt-in", right pole: "No model training on user health data". Keep short.

Triad 2: Shared (E1, E5): cloud processing. Distinct (E4): local-only. So left pole: "Cloud-based processing", right pole: "On-device processing only".

Triad 3: Shared (E1, E3): no training. Distinct (E5): opt-in training. So left pole: "Prohibit training on health data", right pole: "Permit training with user opt-in". But I used similar for Triad 1, just reversed. Triad 3 is essentially the same as Triad 1 but with E3 instead of E2. So I can reuse the phrasing but ensure it's distinct. I'll phrase Triad 1: "Opt-in data use for training" vs "No data use for training"; Triad 3: "No training on health data" vs "Training with explicit opt-in". Actually, I'll make them precise.

Now, output format exactly as specified: no markdown, just plain text. So:

Triad 1:
Left pole: Opt-in training allowed
Right pole: No training on user data

Triad 2:
Left pole: Cloud processing
Right pole: Local-only processing

Triad 3:
Left pole: No training on health data
Right pole: Training with user consent

I'll ensure under 12 words each. Those are short. Provide final answer.
